preFix.title: Cornish fairings
preFix.subtitle: Crisp, spiced ginger biscuits from Cornwall
preFix.excerpt: Cornish fairings are ginger biscuits that crack across the top as they bake, leaving a rough, cratered surface and a strongly spiced crumb. They take their name from the fairgrounds and markets of Cornwall where they were traditionally sold. The dough is quick and the flavour forward: ginger, mixed spice, and a back-note of treacle.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cornish fairings are documented as a regional Cornish speciality sold at fairs and markets from at least the eighteenth century. Recipes appear in Cornish domestic cookery records from the mid-nineteenth century onward, and the biscuit gained protected geographical indication status. The characteristic crack comes from a high proportion of raising agent meeting a firm dough in a hot oven. The spice blend of ginger, mixed spice, and sometimes cinnamon is consistent across historical recipes.

content.text: The dough is on the dry side compared with other biscuit doughs: it comes together into a firm ball that can be rolled between the palms without sticking. Do not add more liquid. The cracks that appear across the top as they bake are caused by the raising agents pushing through the firm dough surface. This is the right result. The biscuits look underdone when they come out but firm up completely as they cool.

Heat the oven to 190 degrees C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Sift 175 g plain flour, 75 g caster sugar, 2 tsp ground ginger, 1 tsp mixed spice, 1 tsp bicarbonate of soda, and half a teaspoon of baking powder into a bowl. Rub in 75 g cold diced butter until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs. Warm 2 tablespoons of golden syrup and 1 teaspoon of black treacle gently in a small pan until just liquid. Pour into the flour mixture and mix with a fork, then bring together with your hands into a firm dough. Roll into balls the size of a walnut and place on the trays with 5 cm between each. Do not flatten. Bake for 10 to 13 minutes until cracked on top and deep amber. Leave on the tray for 5 minutes to firm.

items.solution: The raising agents may be old and lost their potency, or the oven was too cool. Use fresh bicarbonate of soda and baking powder, and check the oven temperature with a thermometer.

items.problem: Biscuits are very hard rather than crisp
items.solution: They were left in the oven too long. Remove when they are a deep amber colour and still look slightly underdone in the centre; they firm up as they cool. Store in an airtight tin to keep the texture.
